interest: Reform-implementation realists and evidence-driven policy designers have an interest in ensuring S3's predicted equity gains are attributed to the full causal bundle, so the policy is not adopted as a single-lever structural change stripped of the teacher-policy and support components that actually produced Finland's outcomes.
value: Fidelity of evidence (correct causal attribution) is in tension with the political appeal of a simple, low-cost structural intervention.
fear: The anticipated harm is that Hungary copies only the cheap, visible structural change (delaying selection) while omitting the costly teacher stabilization, retraining, and early-support infrastructure, and thereby harms the very learners the reform claims to help.
affected: pupils in comprehensive upper grades (esp. lower-SES learners)
assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that the equity effect is separable into components and that the non-structural components (teacher policy + support) are causally necessary rather than merely correlated with Finland's structural design.
empirical_uncertainty: The bundling claim is contested-to-supported in the comparative literature (multiple confounded co-reforms make single-factor attribution genuinely hard), and for Hungary specifically the within-decade equity gain is asserted rather than evidenced, while the teacher-supply shortfall is a documented strong binding constraint.
